scope: Walk QUICKSTART.md + scaffolded README line-by-line. For every DX promise: locate the code path, execute it (or trace a real call), confirm the behaviour matches. Re-verify C1-C4 from pass-1. Hunt the same 'advertised-feature-that-doesn't-work' pattern the critics applied to compromise lies, but at the user-facing TS layer.
summary: Pass-1 criticals C1 (npm run dev) and C2 (npm run build + @types/node) are fully closed — the scaffolder's `scaffolder_produces_working_project` test now runs `npm install && npm test && npm run build && npm run dev` all to green on a clean tmp dir in 82s, and `@types/node ^22.0.0` is in the template devDependencies. C3 partially closed — `PolicyKind.GrantBacked` is now exposed, `grantCapability` / `revokeCapability` / `callAs` are wired through the TS wrapper, and the grant-policy path enforces denials. C4 closed — `Engine.trace` now runs in trace-mode and does NOT persist, observed via `countNodesWithLabel` before/after. But the 'verify-don't-trust-docs' pass surfaces SIX new criticals and four new majors, all of the same shape: a doc / docstring / README example promises a behaviour the code does not deliver. Most severe: (1) the QUICKSTART.md capability example advertises `capability: 'store:post:*'` + grant with the same scope, which the engine silently normalizes to `store:post:write` on the check side and thus denies — the exact example in the flagship docs fails closed; (2) `crud('post').actions` advertises 'update' but `engine.call(h.id, 'post:update', ...)` throws `E_NOT_FOUND: unknown crud op: update`; (3) `engine.trace` is fabricated — every step shares the same `nodeCid`, every step's `durationUs` is `total_elapsed / n`, and the primitive list is a hardcoded lookup keyed off the op name (not a walk of the real subgraph); (4) capability-denied responses return `{ok: false, errorCode: 'E_CAP_DENIED'}` but the TS wrapper does NOT throw — silently swallows the denial; (5) trace `inputs` / `outputs` typed on the TS side are never populated, pass-1 M2 unmoved; (6) native `handlerToMermaid` renders 2 nodes out of 11 for a `crud('post')` handler, pass-1 M1 unmoved. Pattern: the DSL wrapper mutated in the C3 pass covered METHOD existence (23/23 methods wrapped) but not SEMANTIC correctness of those methods.

pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-C1_npm_run_dev_fails: CLOSED — verified at HEAD. `scaffolder_produces_working_project` executes `npm run dev` in tmp dir against `.benten/{name}.redb`; pass. `Engine.open()` gained `ensureParentDir(path)` at packages/engine/src/engine.ts:244 (recursive mkdir of dirname); no more `I/O error: No such file or directory`.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-C2_npm_run_build_fails_no_types_no: CLOSED — template/package.json:16 carries `"@types/node": "^22.0.0"`. scaffolder.test.ts:43 now runs `npm run build` after `npm test` — future regressions caught.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-C3_grantbacked_invisible_from_ts: PARTIAL — surface closed (PolicyKind.GrantBacked exposed, Engine.openWithPolicy / grantCapability / revokeCapability / callAs all wired; smoke-tested: grant succeeds, denial fires). NOT CLOSED: multiple DX gaps in the capability experience remain — see r6b-C1, r6b-C2, r6b-C3, r6b-C6 below. The methods exist but the user-facing story from the docs doesn't actually work end-to-end.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-C4_trace_side_effects: CLOSED — verified with `countNodesWithLabel('post')` before/after a `trace(post:create)` call: count stays 0. Engine::trace passes `true` for trace-mode at engine.rs:710, buffered writes are discarded.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M1_native_mermaid_worse_than_TS: STILL OPEN — reproduced at HEAD. `handler.toMermaid()` for `crud('post')` renders 2 nodes (`crud_post_write`, `respond_1`) with 1 edge; pure TS `toMermaid(h.subgraph)` renders 11 nodes (BRANCH + 5 cases × 2 nodes each) with 10 labeled edges. The wrapper still prefers native (engine.ts:195); pass-1 fix did not land. Now downgraded to major r6b-M4 because the Phase-1 smoke gate `mermaid_output_parses` only checks structural regex and passes on the 2-node output — the lossy mermaid is shipping.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M2_trace_missing_inputs_outputs: STILL OPEN — bindings/napi/src/trace.rs:14-28 emits only `nodeCid`, `durationUs`, `primitive`. The TS `TraceStep.inputs` / `.outputs` fields (types.ts:103-105) are declared and always `undefined`. QUICKSTART.md:88 still promises `{ node, inputs, outputs, durationUs }`. Promoted to r6b-C5 because it's now part of a bigger trace-fabrication story.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M3_trace_step_durations_identical: STILL OPEN and WORSE THAN THOUGHT — engine.rs:732 explicitly computes `per_step = elapsed / n` and assigns the same number to every step (this is intentional in the code, not a bug in the timer). Plus every step shares the SAME nodeCid (the outcome's `created_cid`, not the actual subgraph-node CID). Promoted to r6b-C5 as part of the trace-fabrication critical.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M4_error_catalog_ctx_registerSubgr: CLOSED — docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md:443 now reads `engine.registerSubgraph(handler)` or `engine.registerSubgraph(crud('<label>'))`. errors.generated.ts:897 matches. The codegen pipeline caught up.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M5_ts_engine_only_6_of_23_methods: CLOSED — verified at HEAD: `updateNode`, `deleteNode`, `createEdge`, `getEdge`, `deleteEdge`, `edgesFrom`, `edgesTo`, `callAs`, `grantCapability`, `revokeCapability`, `createView`, `readView`, `emitEvent`, `countNodesWithLabel`, `changeEventCount`, `ivmSubscriberCount`, `metricsSnapshot`, `capabilityWritesCommitted`, `capabilityWritesDenied`, `openWithPolicy` all present. 23/23 coverage. (Note: `handlerPredecessors` still a stub — see pass-1 M7 / r6b-M2.)
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M6_quickstart_honest_status_stale: CLOSED — QUICKSTART.md:97-114 now carries a fresh 'as of 2026-04-17' section with `[x]` marks for items 1-5 and `[ ]` for items 6+ (dev-server HMR, version chains, P2P sync, UCAN, WASM SANDBOX). Accurate.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-M7_handlerPredecessors_vacuous_noo: STILL OPEN — engine.ts:586-598 unchanged; method returns an empty table. Demoted to major r6b-M2 because no new tests rely on it, but it's still a cavity waiting to deepen.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-m1_scaffolder_README_capability_ex: PARTIALLY CLOSED — `CrudOptions.capability` now exists (dsl.ts:539), so the compile failure is resolved. The RUNTIME failure is NEW — see r6b-C1. The code example in the README compiles; it just silently fails closed at runtime.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-m2_engine_close_is_noop: UNCHANGED — engine.ts:291-294 still a marker-only close(); no native close wired. JSDoc at :282-290 now warns callers to avoid in-process re-open. Acceptable as a minor for Phase 1.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-m3_emitEvent_throws_E_PRIMITIVE_NO: UNCHANGED but now WRAPPED — engine.ts:851-861 wraps the native throw through mapNativeError, good. Docstring at :846-850 honestly says Phase 2.
pass1_dispositions.r6-dx-m5_no_help_output_from_scaffolder: UNCHANGED — `node bin.mjs --help` still exits 1 with the 'missing project name' die message. Low cost to fix; still open.

new_criticals.title: QUICKSTART.md capability example silently denies every write — `capability: 'store:post:*'` is normalized to `store:post:write` on the enforcement side but not on the grant-match side
new_criticals.where: docs/QUICKSTART.md:53-61 (the capability example) + the Rust-side scope derivation in the capability-check path
new_criticals.verified_by: Ran the exact QUICKSTART snippet against HEAD (`PolicyKind.GrantBacked` + `crud('post', { capability: 'store:post:*' })` + `grantCapability({ actor: 'alice', scope: 'store:post:*' })` + `engine.call(h.id, 'post:create', ...)`). Observed response: `{edge:'ON_DENIED', errorCode:'E_CAP_DENIED', errorMessage:'capability denied (required=store:post:write, entity=post)'}`. `capabilityWritesDenied()` returned `{ 'store:post:write': 1 }`. Changing the grant scope to the exact string `'store:post:write'` (while the handler still declares `'store:post:*'`) makes the call succeed. Conclusion: the engine's check-side derives the required scope from the entity label as `store:<label>:write`, IGNORING the user-supplied `requires:` on the WRITE Node, and the grant-side stores the user-supplied scope verbatim — so the only grants that ever succeed are ones that happen to use the engine's canonical form. The wildcard example in the flagship doc is guaranteed to fail closed.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: This is the exact DX-lie pattern the new methodology asks to hunt for. QUICKSTART.md is the first doc linked from the README; 'Adding capabilities' is a three-line code block; a developer pastes it and their writes silently vanish with a cryptic `E_CAP_DENIED` message referencing a scope they never wrote. The scaffolder template README (line 34) makes the same promise with `capability: 'store:post:write'` — which happens to work because it matches the normalized form by accident. This is indistinguishable from fraud-by-documentation: the capability subsystem does something real, but the only way to discover the actual shape is to read the Rust source.
new_criticals.fix: Three options. (a) Make the check-side honor the user-supplied `requires:` on the WRITE node — that's what the DSL writes, it's what the handler declared, it's what the developer understood they were granting. (b) Document the normalization scheme in docs/QUICKSTART.md + docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md (E_CAP_DENIED): explicitly say 'the check uses store:<label>:write; your grant scope must match that'. (c) Implement glob-matching so `store:post:*` grants satisfy `store:post:write` checks. I recommend (a)+(c): the user's written intent should be the source of truth, and wildcards should work as documented. Either way, until this lands, revise QUICKSTART.md to use `'store:post:write'` as the example.

new_criticals.title: `crud('post').actions` advertises 'update' but `engine.call(h.id, 'post:update', ...)` throws `E_NOT_FOUND: unknown crud op: update` — 1 of 5 advertised actions is non-functional
new_criticals.where: crates/benten-engine/src/engine.rs — the crud op dispatcher + dsl.ts:573 (`crud(...)` return's .actions array)
new_criticals.verified_by: Against HEAD: `const h = await engine.registerSubgraph(crud('post'))`, then `engine.call(h.id, 'post:create', { title: 'x' })` succeeds. Then `engine.call(h.id, 'post:update', { cid, patch: { title: 'y' } })` throws `E_NOT_FOUND: unknown crud op: update`. `handler.actions` returns `['create', 'get', 'list', 'update', 'delete']`. `engine.trace(h.id, 'post:update', ...)` also throws `E_NOT_FOUND: unknown crud op: update`, confirming the op is unrecognized by the dispatcher (not just a runtime error during execution). The DSL's BRANCH at dsl.ts:617-626 builds an 'update' case, but `registerCrud(label)` (the native fast-path invoked at engine.ts:338) uses a canonical CRUD subgraph, not the DSL-built one, and that canonical shape apparently omits update.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: Every surface in Phase 1 claims 5 CRUD actions work. The scaffolded smoke test (`three_creates_list_returns_them`) exercises create + list but not update, so the regression has been silent. README/QUICKSTART/DSL-SPEC all show `crud()` as the zero-config path. The scaffolder's generated `test/smoke.test.ts` asserts `handler.actions` contains 'update' (template/test/smoke.test.ts:39) but then never calls it. This is the same test-for-existence-not-semantics hazard flagged in R4b.
new_criticals.fix: (a) Short-term: add an `update` branch to the native canonical crud subgraph (engine.rs crud handler synthesis) — the DSL already builds a valid update WRITE case, so the Rust side just needs to recognize `op_name == 'update'` in the dispatcher and route to the WRITE case. (b) Add a sixth smoke gate: `crud_all_five_actions_dispatchable` that invokes each action name from `handler.actions` and asserts no `E_NOT_FOUND`. (c) Stop treating `handler.actions` as a cosmetic property — it's a contract. Any action in that list that errors `E_NOT_FOUND` is a lie.

new_criticals.title: Capability-denied responses silently return `{ok: false, errorCode: 'E_CAP_DENIED'}` instead of throwing — TS wrapper swallows the error
new_criticals.where: packages/engine/src/engine.ts:973-997 (`flattenCallResult`) — the `ok !== false` gate at line 980 falls through to line 996 which returns the raw denial payload
new_criticals.verified_by: With `PolicyKind.GrantBacked` and NO grants seeded, called `engine.call(h.id, 'post:create', { title: 'denied' })`. No exception thrown. Return value: `{ edge:'ON_DENIED', errorCode:'E_CAP_DENIED', errorMessage:'capability denied (required=store:post:write, entity=post)', ok:false, successfulWriteCount:0, createdAt:<ms> }`. `countNodesWithLabel('post')` returned 0 afterward — the write was indeed not persisted. A developer who reads `r.cid` sees `undefined` (no crash) and may not realize anything went wrong.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: The Phase-1 error-contract story is 'every error goes through `mapNativeError` and emerges as a typed subclass of `BentenError` with `code` and `fixHint`'. Cap denials are the single most important runtime error class for anyone using `PolicyKind.GrantBacked` — and they bypass the error contract entirely. The `errorCode` field in the return payload is a ghost of what should be a thrown `ECapDenied`. The scaffolder README markets cap enforcement as `.call(...)` after `.grantCapability(...)`; a developer who doesn't grant (or misgrants) sees no error. Silently dropping writes is the worst possible failure mode for a database.
new_criticals.fix: In `flattenCallResult`, when `raw.ok === false` AND `raw.errorCode` is a string, construct the matching typed error class from `errors.generated.ts` (already keyed by `ERROR_CODES` const) and throw it. Route through `mapNativeError` for consistency. Add a test: `cap_denied_throws_typed_error` that grants nothing, calls, and asserts `err.code === 'E_CAP_DENIED'`. This is a two-line fix plus a test.

new_criticals.title: `engine.trace` is fabricated — step list is hardcoded per-op, all steps share the same `nodeCid`, all steps share the same `durationUs`
new_criticals.verified_by: Against HEAD: `engine.trace(h.id, 'post:create', { title: 'x' })` returned `[{nodeCid:'bafyr4igzdgi...mqf4', primitive:'write', durationUs:41}, {nodeCid:'bafyr4igzdgi...mqf4', primitive:'respond', durationUs:41}]`. Identical `nodeCid` (both are the outcome's `created_cid`). Identical durations. Source inspection at engine.rs:717-730 confirms: op_name drives a `match` to a hardcoded primitive-name vector (e.g. `'create' => vec!["write", "respond"]`), `step_cid` is the terminal `outcome.created_cid` reused for every step, `per_step = elapsed / n` is the same value for every step. There is no per-primitive instrumentation, no per-step timing, no per-step I/O capture, no walk of the actual subgraph.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: This is the biggest lie in the Phase-1 DX surface. QUICKSTART.md:88 promises per-step `{ node, inputs, outputs, durationUs }`. The TS type (types.ts:94-108) declares all four fields. The return shape has two of them populated with fabricated values. Someone using `trace()` to find the slow primitive in a handler will get a wrong answer — every primitive reports the same time. Someone using `trace()` to see the READ's output will get `undefined`. Someone using `trace()` to verify which Nodes were touched will see the same CID over and over. The feature doesn't do what it claims. Pass-1 noted M2 (missing I/O) and M3 (identical durations) separately; the combined picture is that the trace is a post-hoc narrative, not a real tracer.
new_criticals.fix: The right fix is to instrument the evaluator to emit a real `TraceStep` per primitive invocation: `(node_cid_of_the_subgraph_node, primitive_kind, input_binding_snapshot, output_binding_snapshot, elapsed_before_and_after_this_step)`. This is a meaningful Phase-1 scope expansion but the Phase-1 exit criterion (`trace_non_zero_timing`) is currently passing on a fabrication. At minimum, short-term: (a) Walk the handler's subgraph and emit real per-node `nodeCid`s rather than reusing the outcome CID. (b) Capture `Instant::now()` before/after each primitive execution in the evaluator loop and emit per-step deltas. (c) Mark `inputs` / `outputs` as `Optional` in the Rust TraceStep and surface as `null` when unavailable so the TS type is honest. (d) Update QUICKSTART.md:88 + types.ts:94 to describe exactly what is emitted versus what is aspirational.

new_criticals.title: Scaffolded README `callAs` capability example throws `E_CID_PARSE` — `engine.grantCapability({actor: 'alice'})` succeeds but `engine.callAs(h.id, op, input, 'alice')` rejects the string
new_criticals.where: tools/create-benten-app/template/README.md:32-37 + packages/engine/src/engine.ts:740-764 (`Engine.callAs`) + the CID-validation layer on the Rust side
new_criticals.verified_by: Ran the exact scaffolder README snippet (README.md:32-37) verbatim: `const engine = await Engine.openWithPolicy(...)`, `const h = await engine.registerSubgraph(crud('post', { capability: 'store:post:write' }))`, `await engine.grantCapability({ actor: 'alice', scope: 'store:post:write' })` — all succeed. Then `await engine.callAs(h.id, 'post:create', { title: 'x' }, 'alice')` throws `E_CID_PARSE: invalid CID: wrong length`. Inspection of crates/benten-engine/tests/integration/caps_crud.rs:49 shows the correct pattern: `let actor = engine.create_principal('alice').unwrap()` — a CID is derived from the name and THAT CID is passed to `callAs`. The `create_principal` helper is not wrapped in the TS binding and not referenced anywhere in the TS DSL docs. The scaffolder's README example is therefore non-functional as written.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: Two DX failures. (1) `grantCapability({ actor: 'alice', ... })` silently accepts any string — so developers think 'alice' is the right shape. The capability-grant Node is written with `actor: 'alice'` as a raw string. (2) When the check path runs, it parses the grant Node's actor as a CID and presumably the equality check against the caller's actor-CID fails — but I observed that `engine.call` (without explicit actor) succeeds with GrantBacked when a grant exists, so the default-actor path doesn't require CID parsing. The surface is therefore internally inconsistent: `call()` allows string actors (implicitly, via default), `callAs()` requires CIDs. Neither is documented. The scaffolder README's example uses the path that doesn't work.
new_criticals.fix: (a) Wrap `create_principal` through the TS Engine surface: `engine.createPrincipal(name: string): Promise<string>` returning the CID. (b) Update scaffolder README to show `const aliceCid = await engine.createPrincipal('alice'); await engine.grantCapability({ actor: aliceCid, scope: ... }); await engine.callAs(h.id, op, input, aliceCid)`. (c) Have `grantCapability` validate its `actor` argument either as a CID or as a principal-name-that-will-be-derived; reject ambiguous strings. (d) Have `callAs` accept EITHER a CID or a principal name and resolve appropriately — matches developer mental model.

new_criticals.title: Trace's per-step `nodeCid` is the terminal outcome's CID, not the CIDs of the actual subgraph nodes — Mermaid + trace cannot be cross-referenced
new_criticals.verified_by: Observed directly: `trace(post:create)` returns two steps, both with `nodeCid: bafyr4igzdgiycsqykc3wmz6facze4sun26o23f5qrshpw2i7rbk7hkmqf4` — which is the CID of the newly-created post Node, not the CID of the write-primitive subgraph node or the respond-primitive subgraph node. The TS type `TraceStep.nodeCid` is docstring'd as 'Content-addressed CID of the evaluated subgraph Node' (types.ts:97). Native tracer emits the Outcome CID instead.
new_criticals.why_it_matters: The intended DX flow is: dump mermaid → see the subgraph Nodes with CIDs → trace a call → see which Nodes fired, in order, with timings → click through. With the current implementation, `trace.steps.map(s => s.nodeCid)` returns a list of identical CIDs pointing at the created-post — not at any of the primitive Nodes in the subgraph. The two views can't be cross-referenced. This compounds C4: the trace is wrong on timing AND on identity.
new_criticals.fix: Same as C4 fix (d): instrument the evaluator to emit per-primitive-node CIDs. The registered SubgraphSpec carries the per-Node CIDs already (they're content-addressed); the trace just needs to report the Node being evaluated rather than the output.

new_majors.title: `create_principal` / principal-management is Rust-only — no TS surface for the primary identity primitive
new_majors.where: crates/benten-engine tests use `engine.create_principal(name)` extensively; bindings/napi/src/* does not expose it; packages/engine/src/engine.ts does not wrap it
new_majors.fix: Add `createPrincipal(name: string): Promise<string>` to napi bindings + TS wrapper. Return the derived CID. Document in scaffolder README. See r6b-C5.

new_majors.title: `handlerPredecessors` still a vacuous no-op returning empty adjacencies (pass-1 M7 unchanged)
new_majors.fix: Either wire a native `handlerPredecessors(handlerId)` method (the evaluator knows the edges from the registered SubgraphSpec) OR derive the adjacency locally from `handler.subgraph.nodes[].edges` — the `RegisteredHandler.subgraph` is already public. Pick one and delete the stub. Tests relying on this helper are currently passing without asserting anything.

new_majors.title: `Engine.call` return shape drift — list items look different from get items + error payloads mix into success returns
new_majors.observed: A `post:get` returns `{ cid, title, body, createdAt, ... }` (flattened properties). A `post:list` returns `{ items: [{title, ...}], edge: 'OK', ok: true, list: [{labels, properties}] }` — both `items` (flattened) and `list` (raw) present. A `post:create` returns `{ cid, createdCid, edge:'OK', ok: true, successfulWriteCount }` — six fields including the Rust-side `createdCid` duplicate of `cid`. A DENIED `post:create` returns `{ edge:'ON_DENIED', errorCode, errorMessage, ok: false, successfulWriteCount: 0, createdAt }` — same shape, `ok: false` discriminant. Developers have to learn which fields are reliable and which are incidental.
new_majors.fix: Define a strict TS type for each action's return shape and have `flattenCallResult` project to it. For list: `{ items: T[] }` only (drop the raw `list`). For get: return the Node projection (no `edge` / `ok`). For create/update/delete: `{ cid, createdAt }`. For denials: throw (see r6b-C3). Document the return shapes in `DSL-SPECIFICATION.md`.

new_majors.title: Native `handlerToMermaid` renders 2 of 11 subgraph Nodes for `crud('post')` (pass-1 M1 unchanged)
new_majors.where: crates/benten-engine Mermaid rendering + packages/engine/src/engine.ts:195 (wrapper prefers native)
new_majors.fix: Pass-1's recommended fix stands: (a) fix the Rust-side `handlerToMermaid` to walk the real registered subgraph (the canonical crud subgraph has BRANCH + 5 cases × 2 nodes each; rendering only 2 is omitting 9), or (b) flip the wrapper preference at engine.ts:195 to use pure TS `toMermaid(handler.subgraph)` first. The pure TS renderer produces the correct 11-node / 10-edge output today. Recommend (b) as Phase-1 ship-fix, (a) as Phase-2 follow-up tracked issue.

new_majors.title: QUICKSTART.md:88 promises `{ node, inputs, outputs, durationUs }` — actual shape is `{ nodeCid, primitive, durationUs }` (no `node` field, no `inputs`, no `outputs`)
new_majors.where: docs/QUICKSTART.md:88 (doc) vs packages/engine/src/types.ts:94-108 (type) vs bindings/napi/src/trace.rs:14-28 (actual emission)
new_majors.fix: (a) Align: rename TraceStep.nodeCid to TraceStep.node OR rename the doc reference. (b) Implement inputs/outputs emission per r6b-C4. (c) Cross-check docs against types against Rust emission in a CI script.

new_majors.title: `engine.emitEvent(...)` throws `E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED` but neither the wrapper JSDoc nor QUICKSTART.md warns developers — they discover it at runtime
new_majors.observed: `emitEvent` is on the TS Engine prototype, typed, exported, never mentioned in QUICKSTART as a deferred feature. A developer who sees it in IDE autocomplete will call it and eat a typed error. The ENGINE-SPEC and TS wrapper both describe Phase-1 as 'EMIT as a standalone primitive is deferred to Phase 2' — that text should live in the JSDoc at :846.
new_majors.fix: Move the native-side Phase-2 deferral note from `bindings/napi/index.d.ts:82-87` into the TS wrapper's JSDoc at `engine.ts:846` so the IDE hover reveals the Phase-2 status before a developer calls the method. Or: `@deprecated Phase 2 — throws E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED in Phase 1` as a JSDoc annotation so autocomplete strikes it through.

new_minors.title: `--help` / `-h` still fails with 'missing project name' die message instead of printing usage
new_minors.fix: 5 lines — check `args[0] === '--help' || args[0] === '-h'` before the `startsWith('--')` check and print the 'Done. Next steps:' banner-ish usage block. Pass-1 m5 unchanged.

new_minors.title: `TraceStep.node_cid` versus `TraceStep.nodeCid` vs `TraceStep.node` naming drift — three spellings across three files
new_minors.where: engine.ts:561 accepts both `s.nodeCid ?? s.node_cid` — defensive coercion because the native side uses camelCase (trace.rs:17) but the docstring at QUICKSTART.md:88 says `node`
new_minors.fix: Pick one — recommend `nodeCid` — and update QUICKSTART.md:88. Drop the defensive `?? s.node_cid` branch once the Rust side is stable.

new_minors.title: `Engine.countNodesWithLabel` and `Engine.ivmSubscriberCount` are wrapped but not documented in QUICKSTART or DSL-SPEC
new_minors.fix: Add a 'Observability' section to QUICKSTART.md listing the engine's read-only introspection methods (`metricsSnapshot`, `countNodesWithLabel`, `ivmSubscriberCount`, `changeEventCount`, `capabilityWritesCommitted`, `capabilityWritesDenied`). These are load-bearing for debugging but currently undocumented.

new_minors.title: scaffolder `.benten/<name>.redb` path leaks into tests — no `.gitignore` in generated project to exclude it
new_minors.fix: Add `.gitignore` to template with at minimum `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `.benten/`. Developers running `npm run dev` in a scaffolded project will otherwise commit the local DB file by accident.

verify_dont_trust_docs_summary: Ran the QUICKSTART.md install flow (`npx create-benten-app` → `npm install` → `npm run dev`) against HEAD: now passes.
Ran QUICKSTART.md `crud('post')` example + create + list: passes for create/get/list; update throws E_NOT_FOUND (C2).
Ran QUICKSTART.md capability example (`capability: 'store:post:*'` + grant): fails — denials fire on every write (C1).
Ran QUICKSTART.md `engine.trace()`: returns fabricated step list with fake CIDs + identical durations + missing inputs/outputs (C4/C6/M5).
Ran scaffolded README `callAs('alice')`: throws E_CID_PARSE because string actors are CID-parsed (C5).

positive_notes: Pass-1 C1 (dev) and C2 (build) fully closed and verified. `scaffolder_produces_working_project` covers install + test + build + dev.
Pass-1 M4 (error catalog drift) closed. Pass-1 M5 (TS 6-of-23 methods) closed — wrapper now exposes 23/23. Pass-1 M6 (QUICKSTART stale) closed.
Pass-1 C4 (trace side-effects) closed with real trace-mode in the evaluator that discards buffered writes.

Capability subsystem DOES enforce — denials fire, counters increment, grants/revokes work — just not through the surface the docs advertise. The engine machinery is solid; the DX layer over it is the gap.
`.benten/` auto-creation in `Engine.open` is the right DX choice and eliminates an entire class of first-run error.
top_dx_gap_remaining: The capability story is STILL not usable from the docs. Pass-1 C3 was 'no TS surface'; that's fixed. R6b reveals that the TS surface exists but the docs' capability example fails closed (C1), the scaffolded README's capability example throws a CID-parse error (C5), the denial path silently swallows errors (C3), and one of the advertised crud actions is non-functional (C2). The combined DX picture is: a developer following the flagship docs cannot successfully call a single capability-gated crud action end-to-end. Fixing C1 (wildcard matching OR honoring user-supplied `requires`), C2 (crud update dispatch), C3 (throw on denial), and C5 (wrap createPrincipal + fix callAs example) are all surgical — each is 1-3 files — but they compound. Without them, Phase-1's third advertised feature set (capabilities) is non-functional from the DSL. The methodology shift caught these exactly as intended: docstrings and READMEs advertise them as present; executing the code proves them absent.
recommended_action: REVISE. Fix order: (1) r6b-C2 crud update dispatch — 30 min, unblocks `handler.actions` honesty. (2) r6b-C3 denial throws typed error — 10 min + test, closes the silent-failure hazard. (3) r6b-C5 + r6b-M1 wrap createPrincipal + rewrite scaffolder README capability snippet — 1 hour. (4) r6b-C1 either implement wildcard matching OR honor user-supplied `requires:` OR rewrite QUICKSTART example — 2-4 hours depending on direction chosen. (5) r6b-C4 + r6b-C6 + r6b-M5 real trace instrumentation — Phase 1.5 scope, at minimum emit real per-primitive nodeCids (not the outcome CID) and per-step durations; inputs/outputs can be Phase 2 with doc alignment. (6) r6b-M4 flip mermaid preference to TS renderer — 1 line. All new majors + minors can fold into a single R6b fix-pass commit.
